text.title: Freelancer Niche Positioning Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules produce a freelancer one-liner that survives platform search and human skim, with external validation gates before any rollout.

rule.statement: The positioning is exactly one sentence of the form "I do X (concrete service) for Y (named buyer role or industry) so they Z (named outcome)." X is a verb-plus-noun phrase, Y is searchable on the platforms the freelancer uses, Z is an outcome the buyer would say themselves. Multi-sentence positioning is rejected; bullet lists are rejected.

rationale.source: Single-person service business needs a one-line frame that survives Upwork search and LinkedIn skim

rule.rationale: A two-sentence positioning forces the buyer to decide whether to read sentence two before committing — they don't. The X/Y/Z one-liner forces every word to earn its place.

rule.statement: The one-liner must satisfy four constraints simultaneously: (a) search-findable — the buyer's likely platform query contains at least one verbatim word from the line; (b) role-specific — Y names a buyer role or industry, never "businesses" or "startups"; (c) outcome-claimed — Z is a measurable or visible result, not an internal activity ("ship features faster" yes, "write clean code" no); (d) defensible in 60 seconds — the operator can give a coherent example for X, Y, and Z without hedging.

rationale.source: One-line 'I do X for Y' framing that survives Upwork search and LinkedIn skim

rule.rationale: Each constraint kills a specific failure: vague verbs (a) fail to surface in search, vague buyers (b) attract unqualified leads, vague outcomes (c) make the line feel generic, vague defence (d) collapses on the first discovery call.

rule.statement: Before public rollout, three external validation gates must pass: (i) search-find — at least 3 of 5 representative platform searches the buyer is likely to run surface the new profile in the first two pages; (ii) peer recall — 2 of 3 peers, shown the one-liner once for 5 seconds then asked to recall, can name both Y (the buyer) and Z (the outcome); (iii) buyer paraphrase — at least 2 prospects in a discovery call paraphrase the line back accurately when asked "what do you think I do?".

rationale.source: One-liner must survive search and skim — external validation, not self-judgement

rule.rationale: Operators always think their own positioning is clear; external gates are the only way to find out it isn't. Three gates of different shapes (machine search, peer skim, buyer recall) triangulate effectiveness across the surfaces it has to work on.

rule.statement: Rollout is staged across owned surfaces in a fixed order so failures are detected before they become global: (1) site hero copy and one cold-email subject test, (2) LinkedIn headline + about-section, (3) platform profile (Upwork / Toptal / Contra), (4) all secondary surfaces (email signature, business card, conference bio). The operator waits at least 14 days between (2) and (3) to gather signal before changing platform algorithms.

rationale.source: Solo freelancer rollout discipline — surfaces have different cost-to-revert

rule.rationale: Site hero is cheap to revert, platform profiles are not — Upwork's search re-indexes slowly and a wrong one-liner can suppress the profile for weeks. Staging the rollout keeps revertability cheap until the line is proven.

rule.statement: The one-liner has an explicit kill rule: if any of (a) qualified inbound leads from new surface drop > 30% over the first 30 days against the prior baseline, (b) discovery-call buyers consistently ask "so what exactly do you do?" after hearing the line, or (c) the operator cannot defend X, Y, or Z in conversation across three attempts — the line is rolled back to the prior baseline and re-drafted, not patched in place. There is no "let's try a small tweak" middle path.

rationale.source: Solo freelancer cannot afford slow degradation of pipeline — explicit rollback rule

rule.rationale: Without a kill rule the operator sticks with a failing one-liner because each new lead "might be the next one". The 30-day, 3-attempt thresholds are the bright lines that force an honest rewrite instead of slow bleed.

example.title: From generalist to validated one-liner
example.code:
Before (generalist): "Senior full-stack developer with 12+ years of experience in modern web."
Draft: "I do Django + Postgres performance work for B2B SaaS CTOs so their checkout p99 drops under 300ms before Black Friday."
Constraint check: (a) search-findable: "Django performance", "Postgres slow query" → yes (b) role-specific: B2B SaaS CTO → yes (c) outcome-claimed: p99 under 300ms by named deadline → yes (d) defensible: 60s example with k6 + EXPLAIN ANALYZE ready → yes
Validation: (i) search-find: 4/5 representative queries surface the profile in page 1-2 (ii) peer recall: 2/3 peers recall "SaaS CTO" and "p99 / Black Friday" (iii) buyer paraphrase: 3 of 4 discovery calls paraphrase accurately
Rollout: Day 1: site hero + 1 cold-email subject A/B Day 14: LinkedIn headline + about Day 28: Upwork profile (delayed deliberately so platform re-index lands on the validated line, not a draft)
30-day check: Qualified inbound up 22%; rollback rule not triggered; one-liner kept.
example.note: The line shipped only after all three external gates passed; the staged rollout kept the platform profile safe from a half-baked version.
